era_context: Alexander invades India and fights the Battle of the Hydaspes against King Porus. This is the climactic military engagement of his eastern campaigns. In Greece, Macedonian hegemony remains secure. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin's power growing. In India, the Nanda Empire remains the dominant power in the Gangetic plain, but Alexander's invasion threatens the northwestern kingdoms.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records from Indian, Persian, and Qin sources

events.title: Battle of the Hydaspes River
events.description: Alexander defeats King Porus of the Paurava kingdom at the Hydaspes River (modern Jhelum River). The battle is one of Alexander's most celebrated victories, fought against an opponent with war elephants—a new military challenge for the Macedonian army. Alexander's tactical brilliance and the discipline of his troops overcome Porus's numerical advantage and the psychological impact of the elephants. The victory opens the way for Alexander's further advance into India and demonstrates the superiority of Macedonian tactics over Indian military organization. Porus is defeated but treated with respect and allowed to retain his kingdom as a vassal.
key_figures: Bucephalus (Alexander's horse, reportedly died after the battle)

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book V
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book IX
Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Book XVII

events.certainty_note: The Battle of the Hydaspes is well-attested in multiple ancient sources and is one of the most reliably documented events of Alexander's campaign. Dating to 326 BCE is accepted by most modern scholars. The battle itself is historically certain, though some tactical details are debated.

events.title: Founding of Bucephala
events.description: Alexander founds the city of Bucephala on the Hydaspes River, named after his famous horse Bucephalus, who reportedly died shortly after the Battle of the Hydaspes. The city is established as a Macedonian settlement and garrison to consolidate control over the region. The founding of Bucephala represents Alexander's policy of establishing Greek cities throughout his empire to serve as centers of Hellenistic culture and administrative control.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book V

events.certainty_note: The founding of Bucephala is attested in ancient sources, but its exact location and subsequent history are uncertain. Archaeological evidence for the city is limited. Dating to 326 BCE is generally accepted, but some sources place it slightly later.

events.title: Alexander Advances into the Gangetic Plain
events.description: Following his victory at the Hydaspes, Alexander advances further into India, moving toward the Gangetic plain. His army encounters reports of the massive armies of the Nanda Empire, which controls the Gangetic region. The prospect of facing an even larger opponent, combined with the exhaustion of his troops and the monsoon season, leads to the army's refusal to advance further. Alexander is forced to turn back, marking the beginning of his retreat from India.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book V
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book IX

events.certainty_note: Alexander's advance into India and the army's refusal to proceed further is well-attested in multiple sources. The event is historically certain, though the exact extent of Alexander's advance and the reasons for the army's refusal are debated.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of the Hydaspes is one of the most reliably documented events of Alexander's campaign, but some modern scholars question the accuracy of the casualty figures and tactical details provided by ancient sources. The founding of Bucephala is attested in sources, but its exact location and subsequent history are uncertain; some scholars question whether it was a major city or a minor garrison. The story of Bucephalus's death after the battle may be legendary rather than historical. The extent of Alexander's advance into India and the reasons for the army's refusal to proceed further are debated; some sources suggest the army was more concerned about supply lines and the monsoon than about the Nanda Empire's armies.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 326 BCE is relatively well-documented compared to earlier years of Alexander's campaign, but still depends entirely on later Greek and Roman sources. Arrian, Plutarch, and Curtius Rufus are the primary sources, all writing centuries after the events. The accounts reflect the perspectives of Alexander's companions and later Hellenistic historians. The Battle of the Hydaspes is one of the most reliably documented events of Alexander's campaign, but some tactical details are debated. Indian sources provide no contemporary account of Alexander's invasion; the Nanda Empire's perspective is entirely absent. Archaeological evidence from northwestern India for this period is limited. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political events; the experiences of conquered populations, economic impacts, and cultural developments are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: Victory at Hydaspes leads to founding of Bucephala
Victory at Hydaspes enables further advance into India
Army's refusal to advance leads to retreat from India
